title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to interact with the interactive network graph generated for this project and provides technical instructions on how to embed the exported Sigma.js HTML file into a Quarto website.

title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to interact with the interactive network graph generated for this project and provides technical instructions on how to embed the exported Sigma.js HTML file into a Quarto website.

title: User Guide
text:
Part 1: Navigating the Interactive Graph
The network graph provides a dynamic view of the Sailor Shift’s influence in the music industry as well as the Oceanus Folk ecosystem. Use the following controls to explore the data:

Basic Navigation

Zooming: Use your mouse scroll wheel or the + and - buttons in the control panel to zoom in and out of the network.
Panning: Click and drag anywhere on the background canvas to move around the graph.
Hovering: Hover over any node (circle) to reveal its label (e.g., Artist Name, Song Title).
Clicking: Click on a specific node to highlight its direct connections (edges) while dimming the rest of the network. Click the background canvas to clear the selection.



Using the Search and Filter Panel
The control panel (typically located on the right or left side of the screen) allows for advanced exploration:

Search by Node: Use the search bar to find a specific artist or song. The graph will center on your search result.
Group Selection: If the data was partitioned in Gephi (e.g., by Node Type or Modularity Class), use the dropdown menus to filter the network to display only specific groups.

title: Prototypes & Iterations
text:
This section will showcase the prototypes of our visual analytics application.
We will include initial sketches, wireframes, and interactive prototypes that we develop throughout the project.

title: Prototypes & Iterations
text:
The Value of Iteration
Visual analytics is rarely a straight line from raw data to a finished dashboard. Throughout this project, several initial prototypes were discarded or heavily iterated upon after testing revealed that they either misrepresented the data or failed to answer the core research questions effectively.
Below is a breakdown of the specific analytical hurdles we faced for Question 1 and Question 3, and how iterating our prototypes led to the final visual solutions.

title: Prototypes & Iterations
section: Iterations for Question 1: Mapping the Ecosystem
text:
Iterations for Question 1: Mapping the Ecosystem
Our goal for Question 1 was to map the internal topology of the Oceanus Folk genre and isolate Sailor Shift’s foundational network. Our approach to visualising this network required three distinct iterations.

Iteration 1: The Tableau Coordinate Approach
Our initial prototype attempted to build the entire Ego Network directly within Tableau. Because Tableau is not a native graph-rendering engine, this required manually engineering a dataset with calculated X and Y coordinates to force Tableau to draw nodes and edges. * The Problem: While technically possible, this prototype resulted in a highly rigid, static visual. The manual coordinate calculations were computationally heavy, and the resulting “hairball” was difficult for a user to navigate or interpret, defeating the purpose of an interactive visual analytics tool.



Iteration 1: Manual Coordinate Network in Tableau




Iteration 2: Tableau Diagram Chart Extension
Realising that manually calculating coordinates was inefficient, we pivoted to using Gephi strictly as a data-filtering engine. We anchored the graph on Sailor Shift, extracted her specific Ego Network, and exported this cleaned, filtered node/edge list back into Tableau. We then utilised a Tableau Diagram/Network Extension to render the graph. * The Problem: While the extension successfully drew the network without requiring manual X/Y coordinates, it introduced severe performance bottlenecks. Tableau dashboard extensions often struggle to render dense, multi-relational graphs smoothly. Furthermore, the extension lacked the native physics layouts (like ForceAtlas2) and deep interactive customisations required to make the hairball legible, resulting in a clunky user experience.
 


Iteration 3 (Final): Sigma.js Interactive Integration
To solve the interactivity problem, we bypassed Tableau entirely for this specific question. We utilised the Sigma.js exporter plugin directly within Gephi. * The Solution: This generated a self-contained, lightweight HTML/JavaScript application that preserved all of Gephi’s structural math while adding dynamic user controls (zooming, panning, and click-to-highlight). We then embedded this interactive widget directly into our Quarto website as our final solution for Question 1.

title: Prototypes & Iterations
section: Iterations for Question 3: Predicting Rising Stars
text:
Iterations for Question 3: Predicting Rising Stars
Question 3 required us to define the characteristics of a rising star and predict the next industry leaders. This section required the most significant pivot of the entire project, as our initial mathematical hypothesis proved to be flawed.

Iteration 1: The Structural Network Hypothesis
Our initial assumption was that “influence” is best measured by network topology. We used Gephi to calculate standard centrality metrics for the entire dataset: * PageRank: To measure elite institutional influence. * In-Degree: To measure how often an artist was covered or sampled. * Out-Degree (Node Size): To measure their historical output.


Iteration 2: The Scatter Plot Prototype
We exported these Gephi metrics into Tableau and built a scatter plot prototype. The goal was to find the “rising stars” by looking for artists with high PageRank and low Out-Degree. * The Problem: When we plotted the data, we discovered a massive “newcomer blindspot.” Brand-new artists were completely isolated in the bottom-left corner of the chart with zero PageRank. We realised that network math is a lagging indicator; because it takes years for other artists to copy a newcomer’s style, purely structural metrics inherently punish emerging talent and heavily favor established veterans.

[Insert your scatter plot screenshot here! This is the crucial visual. Name it: ![Iteration 2: The PageRank Scatter Plot revealing the lagging indicator trap](images/q3_proto1.jpg)]



Iteration 3 (Final): The Momentum Pivot
Because our Gephi prototype proved that structure cannot predict real-time breakouts, we completely scrapped the scatter plot. We pivoted our methodology from “Structural Analytics” to “Momentum Analytics.” * The Solution: Using Tableau, we engineered a temporal dataset that bypassed lagging network edges. Instead, we filtered for artists who debuted recently and ranked them by the sheer volume and velocity of their Recent Notable Works. This final prototype successfully bypassed the structural lag and accurately identified high-momentum newcomers like Min Qin and Carlos Duffy.

[Insert a screenshot of Ewan’s early/unpolished Tableau bar chart here. Name it: ![Iteration 3: Shifting to Momentum Analytics in Tableau](images/q3_proto2.jpg)]

title: Project Poster
text: Poster

title: Visual Analytics Project
text:
The dataset used in this study is derived from Mini-Challenge 1 of the 2025 VAST Challenge. It consists of a synthetic music knowledge graph representing entities and relationships within a fictional but realistic music ecosystem. The data is provided in CSV format and can be modeled as a directed multigraph for network analysis using Python libraries such as networkx.
The dataset is split into two files:

mc1_nodes.csv: stores the graph entities
mc1_edges.csv: stores the relationships between entities





Number of nodes: 17,412
Number of edges: 37,857
Graph type: Directed multigraph
File format: CSV

This structure allows the analysis of artist careers, collaborations, production roles, and influence pathways across the music industry.



The graph includes five distinct node types. Each node has an id attribute that uniquely identifies it in the graph.


Represents individuals in the music industry, such as singers, composers, instrumentalists, lyricists, and producers.
Attributes:

id (integer): Unique node identifier
name (string): Name of the person
stage_name (string, optional): Stage name of the musician




Represents officially organized musical groups such as bands.
Attributes:

id (integer): Unique node identifier
name (string): Name of the group




Represents organizations involved in recording, production, or distribution.
Attributes:

id (integer): Unique node identifier
name (string): Name of the record label




Represents an individual musical work.
Attributes:

id (integer): Unique node identifier
name (string): Song title
genre (string): Genre label
release_date (numeric): Year of release
written_date (numeric, optional): Year the song was written
single (boolean): Whether the song was released as a single
notable (boolean): Whether the song is marked as notable
notoriety_date (numeric, optional): Year the song became notable




Represents a collection of songs released together.
Attributes:

id (integer): Unique node identifier
name (string): Album title
genre (string): Genre label
release_date (numeric): Year of release
written_date (numeric, optional): Year associated with writing or creation
notable (boolean): Whether the album is marked as notable
notoriety_date (numeric, optional): Year the album became notable





Each edge describes a relationship between two nodes. The edge file contains the following attributes:
Attributes:

source (integer): Starting node ID
target (integer): Ending node ID
Edge Type (string): Type of relationship
key (integer): Distinguishes multiple edges between the same pair of nodes

Here, both source and target reference the id field in the node table.
The edge types can be grouped into the following categories:



PerformerOf: A Person or MusicalGroup performed a Song or Album
ComposerOf: A Person composed a Song or Album
LyricistOf: A Person wrote lyrics for a Song or Album
ProducerOf: A Person or RecordLabel contributed to the production of a Song or Album





RecordedBy: A Song or Album was recorded by a RecordLabel
DistributedBy: A Song or Album was distributed by a RecordLabel
MemberOf: A Person is a member of a MusicalGroup





InStyleOf: A work is stylistically influenced by another work or artist
InterpolatesFrom: A work incorporates melodic or musical material from another work
CoverOf: A work is a cover version of an existing work
DirectlySamples: A work directly samples another work
LyricalReferenceTo: A work references another work lyrically






The analysis was carried out systematically to address the three core research questions.




Question 1 examines the internal structure of the Oceanus Folk genre and traces the specific career trajectory of Sailor Shift to determine her roots, collaborators, and indirect creative reach.



The analysis used the music knowledge graph dataset:

mc1_nodes.csv for entity information
mc1_edges.csv for relationships

Relevant node types

Song
Album
Person
MusicalGroup

Relevant edge types To map the internal topology and legacy, the following relationship types were used:

PerformerOf
ComposerOf
CoverOf
LyricalReferenceTo
PartOf




To isolate Sailor Shift’s true referential footprint, the network was filtered using structural graph mathematics in Gephi.
Ego Network Anchoring The graph was anchored on Sailor Shift (Node ID = 17255). To capture all direct and indirect pathways without artificially distorting the community boundaries, the Ego Network Depth was set to MAX.
Intersection Filtering An Intersection Operator was applied to combine the Ego Network with a partition filter (genre = Oceanus Folk). This logic successfully stripped away professional networking noise and non-folk entities, isolating the interconnected core of Oceanus Folk music (Songs and Albums) that directly or indirectly ties to her catalog.




A network graph highlighting the densely interconnected catalog of Oceanus Folk music, distinguishing between the collaborative core and standalone independent tracks.








Question 2 examines how the influence of Oceanus Folk spread across the wider music industry and how the genre itself evolved after the rise of Sailor Shift. The analysis focuses on three sub-questions:

whether Oceanus Folk’s influence spread gradually or intermittently
which genres and artists were most influenced by Oceanus Folk
how Oceanus Folk changed after Sailor Shift’s rise, and which genres became its main contemporary inspirations




The analysis used the same music knowledge graph dataset:

mc1_nodes.csv for entity information
mc1_edges.csv for relationships between entities

Relevant node types

Song
Album
Person
MusicalGroup

Relevant node attributes

id
name
genre
release_date
written_date
notable
notoriety_date
type

Relevant edge types To capture musical influence, the following relationship types were used:

InStyleOf
InterpolatesFrom
CoverOf
DirectlySamples
LyricalReferenceTo

To connect works to artists, the following relationship type was also used:

PerformerOf




Graph structure The edge table was used as the base table, and the node table was joined twice:

once to identify the source work
once to identify the target work

This allowed each influence edge to be interpreted using:

source work genre
target work genre
source work year
target work year

Time variable A unified work year field was created using:

release_date as the primary year
written_date as a fallback
notoriety_date as a second fallback where necessary

This derived year was used as the main temporal variable in all Question 2 visualizations.
Influence filter Only rows belonging to the five influence-related edge types were retained, so that the analysis focused on stylistic borrowing, referencing, and musical reuse rather than production or membership relationships.



The analysis depends on the direction of the influence edges.
Outward influence from Oceanus Folk To identify how Oceanus Folk spread to the wider music world, the following logic was used:

target genre = Oceanus Folk
source genre ≠ Oceanus Folk

This means:

the source work is a non-Oceanus Folk work
the target work is an Oceanus Folk work
therefore, the source work is drawing influence from Oceanus Folk

This outward subset was used for:

influence over time
top influenced genres
top influenced artists

Inward inspiration into Oceanus Folk To identify how Oceanus Folk changed over time, the reverse logic was used:

source genre = Oceanus Folk
target genre ≠ Oceanus Folk

This means:

the Oceanus Folk work is referencing or borrowing from another genre
therefore, the target genre represents a source of inspiration for Oceanus Folk

This inward subset was used to analyze how Oceanus Folk evolved before and after Sailor Shift’s rise.



To evaluate whether Oceanus Folk’s influence spread intermittently or gradually, the outward influence subset was grouped by the source work year.
Measures used

Number of influence mentions: total count of influence edges per year
Number of unique influenced works: distinct count of source works per year
Cumulative unique influenced works: running total of distinct source works over time

Why these measures were used The distinct count of source works was used as the main metric because a single work could contain multiple influence links, which would overstate spread if only raw edge counts were used.
Visualization used

yearly line chart of unique influenced works
cumulative line chart of unique influenced works over time

These charts made it possible to determine whether Oceanus Folk spread through isolated spikes, steady growth, or a hybrid of both.



Genre analysis Using the outward influence subset, the source work genre was grouped and ranked.
Measures used for genres

distinct count of influenced works
raw count of influence mentions
comparison across genres

Why this was done The source genre indicates which genres were borrowing from or referencing Oceanus Folk. Ranking these genres reveals which parts of the music world absorbed Oceanus Folk most strongly.
Artist analysis To identify the artists most influenced by Oceanus Folk, influenced works were linked back to performers using PerformerOf.
Measures used for artists

distinct count of influenced works per artist
recent activity where relevant
notable works as supporting context

Filtering approach

only valid artist-work links were retained
artists were filtered to Person when focusing on solo artists
unique artist identifiers were used to avoid merging different artists with the same name

Visualization used

horizontal bar chart of top influenced genres
horizontal bar chart of top influenced artists

These views answered which genres and artists were most strongly shaped by Oceanus Folk.



To understand how Oceanus Folk evolved, the inward inspiration subset was split into two time periods using Sailor Shift’s breakthrough year.
Turning point used

Before 2028
2028 and after

Why 2028 was used The project brief identifies 2028 as the year of Sailor Shift’s breakthrough, making it the most meaningful historical breakpoint for evaluating change in Oceanus Folk.
Measures used

distinct count of Oceanus Folk works influenced by each target genre
recent notable Oceanus Folk works where needed for supporting analysis
maximum active year for recency checks

Visualization used

side-by-side comparison of inspiration genres before and after 2028
post-2028 ranking chart of top contemporary inspiration genres

Why this approach was used This allowed the analysis to compare which genres shaped Oceanus Folk before Sailor’s rise, which genres became more important afterward, and whether Oceanus Folk became more hybrid and diverse in the contemporary period.



To improve reliability, findings were cross-checked across multiple views:

time-based spread patterns were checked against genre rankings
top influenced genres were compared with top influenced artists
changes in inward inspiration were checked using both before/after comparison and post-2028 ranking

This reduced the risk of relying on a single chart or single metric.



In summary, Question 2 was addressed using a graph-based, directional influence analysis built from the music knowledge graph. The method separated outward influence of Oceanus Folk on other genres and artists, and inward inspiration flowing back into Oceanus Folk. By combining temporal analysis, genre ranking, artist ranking, and pre/post-2028 comparison, the methodology was able to show both how Oceanus Folk spread across the musical world and how the genre itself evolved in the Sailor Shift era.







Question 3 aims to develop a profile of what it means to be a rising star in the music industry and then use that profile to predict the next Oceanus Folk stars over the next five years.
The analysis was divided into two parts:

Part A: Compare the careers of three artists to define the characteristics of a rising star.
Part B: Build a shortlist of recent Oceanus Folk artists and use the rising-star profile to make three predictions.




Artist selection Three artists were selected to represent different career trajectories:

Sailor Shift (17255) as the breakout benchmark
Szymon Pyć (551) as a long, steady-growth artist
Sienna Fox (17012) as a newer high-momentum artist

This combination was used to compare three different pathways to prominence: a rapid breakout trajectory, a long and steady accumulation trajectory, and a recent high-momentum emergence trajectory.
Part A1: Build the artist-career data source A separate artist-career data source was created to connect artists to their works.
Tables used

mc1_edges.csv as Performer Edge
mc1_nodes.csv as Artist Node
mc1_nodes.csv as Work Node

Join logic

Performer Edge.source = Artist Node.id
Performer Edge.target = Work Node.id

This data source was used to study career output over time, popularity growth over time, and Oceanus Folk candidate activity.
Part A2: Create calculated fields for artist-career analysis The following calculated fields were created:

Is Performer Edge: Used to keep only performer relationships. [Edge Type] = "PerformerOf"
Work ID: Used to identify distinct works performed by each artist. [Target]
Work Year: Used to assign a year to each work. The year was taken from release_date first, then written_date, then notoriety_date.
Valid Artist Work: Used to keep only valid artist-work rows.
Notable Work ID: Used to identify works marked as notable. IF UPPER(TRIM([Notable (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)])) = "TRUE" THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Work ID: Used to identify works in the recent period. IF [Work Year] >= 2035 THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Notable Work ID: Used to identify notable works in the recent period.
Artist Name: Used for display.
Artist ID: Used to uniquely identify artists.
Artist Key: Used to avoid merging artists with the same name. [Artist Name] + " (" + STR([Artist ID]) + ")"

Part A3: Build the three comparison visualizations
Visualization 1: Career Output Over Time This chart shows how the number of distinct works accumulated over time for the three selected artists.

Columns: Work Year
Rows: COUNTD(Work ID)
Color: Artist Name
Filter: Valid Artist Work = True
Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox
Table calculation: Running Total This chart was used to compare the scale and pace of each artist’s output over time.

Visualization 2: Popularity Growth Over Time This chart shows how each artist accumulated notable works over time.

Columns: Work Year
Rows: COUNTD(Notable Work ID)
Color: Artist Name
Filter: Valid Artist Work = True
Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox
Table calculation: Running Total

This chart was used as a proxy for popularity growth.
Visualization 3: Influence Growth Over Time This chart shows how often later works referenced each artist’s earlier work. For this visualization, a second data source was required.
Part A4: Build the artist-influence data source A separate artist-influence data source was built to measure when later works referenced each artist’s earlier work.
Tables used

mc1_edges.csv as Influence Edge
mc1_nodes.csv as Source Work
mc1_nodes.csv as Target Work
mc1_edges.csv as Performer Bridge
mc1_nodes.csv as Artist Node

Join logic

Influence Edge.source = Source Work.id
Influence Edge.target = Target Work.id
Influence Edge.target = Performer Bridge.target
Performer Bridge.source = Artist Node.id

This structure links the later referencing work to the earlier referenced work to the performer of that earlier work.
Part A5: Create calculated fields for artist-influence analysis

Is Influence Edge: Used to keep only influence-type relationships.
Is Performer Edge: Used to keep only performer relationships in the bridge table.
Influence Year: Used to assign a year to the later referencing work.
Valid Artist Influence: Used to keep only valid influence rows.
Referencing Work ID: Used to count distinct later works that referenced the artist’s earlier work. [Source]
Artist Key: Used to uniquely identify artists and avoid merging duplicate names.

Part A6: Build the influence chart Visualization 3: Influence Growth Over Time

Columns: Influence Year
Rows: COUNTD(Referencing Work ID)
Color: Artist Key
Filter: Valid Artist Influence = True
Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox
Table calculation: Running Total

This chart was used as a proxy for influence growth over time.



The three comparison charts were sufficient to define the profile of a rising star, but they were not sufficient to predict the next Oceanus Folk stars. A separate shortlist sheet was therefore created using only recent Oceanus Folk artists.
Part B1: Create calculated fields for Oceanus Folk candidate ranking Using the artist-career data source, the following fields were created:

Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" AND [Work Year] >= 2030 THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" AND [Work Year] >= 2030 AND UPPER(TRIM([Notable (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)])) = "TRUE" THEN [Work ID] END
Oceanus Folk Active Year: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" THEN [Work Year] END

Part B2: Build the shortlist table A shortlist sheet called Oceanus Folk Rising Star Candidates was created.
Filters applied

Valid Artist Work = True
Node Type = Person

Rows

Artist Key

Measures shown

MAX(Oceanus Folk Active Year)
COUNTD(Oceanus Folk Work ID)
COUNTD(Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID)
COUNTD(Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID)

Sorting logic Candidates were ranked using the following priority:

COUNTD(Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID) descending
COUNTD(Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID) descending
MAX(Oceanus Folk Active Year) descending

This shortlist was then used to select the final three Oceanus Folk star predictions based on the “High-Momentum” profile.





To support interpretation of the knowledge graph, multiple visualization types were used:

Network graphs to show collaboration and influence relationships
Timelines to show career progression and genre spread over time
Genre comparison charts to show how Oceanus Folk influenced other genres and how it evolved
Artist comparison views to compare rising-star trajectories

text:

Visualization used

horizontal bar chart of top influenced genres
horizontal bar chart of top influenced artists

These views answered which genres and artists were most strongly shaped by Oceanus Folk.



To understand how Oceanus Folk evolved, the inward inspiration subset was split into two time periods using Sailor Shift’s breakthrough year.
Turning point used

Before 2028
2028 and after

Why 2028 was used The project brief identifies 2028 as the year of Sailor Shift’s breakthrough, making it the most meaningful historical breakpoint for evaluating change in Oceanus Folk.
Measures used

distinct count of Oceanus Folk works influenced by each target genre
recent notable Oceanus Folk works where needed for supporting analysis
maximum active year for recency checks

Visualization used

side-by-side comparison of inspiration genres before and after 2028
post-2028 ranking chart of top contemporary inspiration genres

Why this approach was used This allowed the analysis to compare which genres shaped Oceanus Folk before Sailor’s rise, which genres became more important afterward, and whether Oceanus Folk became more hybrid and diverse in the contemporary period.



To improve reliability, findings were cross-checked across multiple views:

time-based spread patterns were checked against genre rankings
top influenced genres were compared with top influenced artists
changes in inward inspiration were checked using both before/after comparison and post-2028 ranking

This reduced the risk of relying on a single chart or single metric.



In summary, Question 2 was addressed using a graph-based, directional influence analysis built from the music knowledge graph. The method separated outward influence of Oceanus Folk on other genres and artists, and inward inspiration flowing back into Oceanus Folk. By combining temporal analysis, genre ranking, artist ranking, and pre/post-2028 comparison, the methodology was able to show both how Oceanus Folk spread across the musical world and how the genre itself evolved in the Sailor Shift era.







Question 3 aims to develop a profile of what it means to be a rising star in the music industry and then use that profile to predict the next Oceanus Folk stars over the next five years.
The analysis was divided into two parts:

Part A: Compare the careers of three artists to define the characteristics of a rising star.
Part B: Build a shortlist of recent Oceanus Folk artists and use the rising-star profile to make three predictions.




Artist selection Three artists were selected to represent different career trajectories:

Sailor Shift (17255) as the breakout benchmark
Szymon Pyć (551) as a long, steady-growth artist
Sienna Fox (17012) as a newer high-momentum artist

This combination was used to compare three different pathways to prominence: a rapid breakout trajectory, a long and steady accumulation trajectory, and a recent high-momentum emergence trajectory.
Part A1: Build the artist-career data source A separate artist-career data source was created to connect artists to their works.
Tables used

mc1_edges.csv as Performer Edge
mc1_nodes.csv as Artist Node
mc1_nodes.csv as Work Node

Join logic

Performer Edge.source = Artist Node.id
Performer Edge.target = Work Node.id

This data source was used to study career output over time, popularity growth over time, and Oceanus Folk candidate activity.
Part A2: Create calculated fields for artist-career analysis The following calculated fields were created:

Is Performer Edge: Used to keep only performer relationships. [Edge Type] = "PerformerOf"
Work ID: Used to identify distinct works performed by each artist. [Target]
Work Year: Used to assign a year to each work. The year was taken from release_date first, then written_date, then notoriety_date.
Valid Artist Work: Used to keep only valid artist-work rows.
Notable Work ID: Used to identify works marked as notable. IF UPPER(TRIM([Notable (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)])) = "TRUE" THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Work ID: Used to identify works in the recent period. IF [Work Year] >= 2035 THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Notable Work ID: Used to identify notable works in the recent period.
Artist Name: Used for display.
Artist ID: Used to uniquely identify artists.
Artist Key: Used to avoid merging artists with the same name. [Artist Name] + " (" + STR([Artist ID]) + ")"

Part A3: Build the three comparison visualizations
Visualization 1: Career Output Over Time This chart shows how the number of distinct works accumulated over time for the three selected artists.

Columns: Work Year
Rows: COUNTD(Work ID)
Color: Artist Name
Filter: Valid Artist Work = True
Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox
Table calculation: Running Total This chart was used to compare the scale and pace of each artist’s output over time.

Visualization 2: Popularity Growth Over Time This chart shows how each artist accumulated notable works over time.

Columns: Work Year
Rows: COUNTD(Notable Work ID)
Color: Artist Name
Filter: Valid Artist Work = True
Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox
Table calculation: Running Total

This chart was used as a proxy for popularity growth.
Visualization 3: Influence Growth Over Time This chart shows how often later works referenced each artist’s earlier work. For this visualization, a second data source was required.
Part A4: Build the artist-influence data source A separate artist-influence data source was built to measure when later works referenced each artist’s earlier work.
Tables used

mc1_edges.csv as Influence Edge
mc1_nodes.csv as Source Work
mc1_nodes.csv as Target Work
mc1_edges.csv as Performer Bridge
mc1_nodes.csv as Artist Node

Join logic

Influence Edge.source = Source Work.id
Influence Edge.target = Target Work.id
Influence Edge.target = Performer Bridge.target
Performer Bridge.source = Artist Node.id

This structure links the later referencing work to the earlier referenced work to the performer of that earlier work.
Part A5: Create calculated fields for artist-influence analysis

Is Influence Edge: Used to keep only influence-type relationships.
Is Performer Edge: Used to keep only performer relationships in the bridge table.
Influence Year: Used to assign a year to the later referencing work.
Valid Artist Influence: Used to keep only valid influence rows.
Referencing Work ID: Used to count distinct later works that referenced the artist’s earlier work. [Source]
Artist Key: Used to uniquely identify artists and avoid merging duplicate names.

Part A6: Build the influence chart Visualization 3: Influence Growth Over Time

Columns: Influence Year
Rows: COUNTD(Referencing Work ID)
Color: Artist Key
Filter: Valid Artist Influence = True
Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox
Table calculation: Running Total

This chart was used as a proxy for influence growth over time.



The three comparison charts were sufficient to define the profile of a rising star, but they were not sufficient to predict the next Oceanus Folk stars. A separate shortlist sheet was therefore created using only recent Oceanus Folk artists.
Part B1: Create calculated fields for Oceanus Folk candidate ranking Using the artist-career data source, the following fields were created:

Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" AND [Work Year] >= 2030 THEN [Work ID] END
Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" AND [Work Year] >= 2030 AND UPPER(TRIM([Notable (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)])) = "TRUE" THEN [Work ID] END
Oceanus Folk Active Year: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" THEN [Work Year] END

Part B2: Build the shortlist table A shortlist sheet called Oceanus Folk Rising Star Candidates was created.
Filters applied

Valid Artist Work = True
Node Type = Person

Rows

Artist Key

Measures shown

MAX(Oceanus Folk Active Year)
COUNTD(Oceanus Folk Work ID)
COUNTD(Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID)
COUNTD(Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID)

Sorting logic Candidates were ranked using the following priority:

COUNTD(Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID) descending
COUNTD(Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID) descending
MAX(Oceanus Folk Active Year) descending

This shortlist was then used to select the final three Oceanus Folk star predictions based on the “High-Momentum” profile.





To support interpretation of the knowledge graph, multiple visualization types were used:

Network graphs to show collaboration and influence relationships
Timelines to show career progression and genre spread over time
Genre comparison charts to show how Oceanus Folk influenced other genres and how it evolved
Artist comparison views to compare rising-star trajectories

title: Analysis and Results
text:
This section will detail the analysis we performed and the results we obtained.
We will present the visualizations we created and discuss the insights we gained from them, addressing the research questions outlined in our project proposal.

1. Sailor Shift Influence Network


The Sailor Shift Influence Network is a directed graph that illustrates the influence relationships between Sailor Shift and other entities that it interacts with. Each node represents an entity, and the directed edges indicate the direction of influence. The size of the nodes corresponds to their degree of influence, while the color coding helps to differentiate differentiate the names of the entities.


(a) Who has she been most influenced by over time?



Most Influenced on Sailor Shift


This graph examines the foundational roots of Sailor Shift’s musical style. It reveals that her record-breaking sound did not emerge in a vacuum; rather, it evolved directly from her early collaborative work. Ivy Echoes stands out as the most significant node in her early network. Rather than being an external ‘older’ influence, this represents her own former band (active 2023–2026). The graph proves that her 2028 solo breakthrough was built heavily upon the stylistic foundation, catalog, and collaborative network she initially developed during her time with the group.


(b) Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?

Professional Network
 While Sailor Shift maintained creative independence, she did not operate entirely on her own. The massive Oceanic Records node stands out as the largest hub in her professional network. The ProducerOf arrow pointing from the label to Sailor Shift reveals that her independent style was supported by major institutional power. Oceanic Records acted as the financial and production engine that distributed her music, giving her the massive platform needed to become a “Super-Hub” and influence the rest of the folk genre.


Directly or Indirectly Influenced
 While her working circle was small, her creative impact was huge. This graph shows a large ring of arrows pointing toward her, representing artists who cover her songs or copy her style. It proves that she became a “Super-Hub”. Hundreds of younger musicians used her sound as a blueprint to build their own careers, even if they never actually met or worked with her.



(c) How has she influenced collaborators of the broader Oceanus Folk community?
 By filtering the network exclusively for the Oceanus Folk genre, at max hops, we reveal the structural backbone of the music itself. This visualisation maps how Sailor Shift’s catalogue is woven into the broader discography of the genre.

The Tangled Core: The dense web of connected nodes represents a highly referential body of work. Within the Oceanus Folk genre, songs and albums are deeply intertwined through covers, lyrical references, and shared album structures. Sailor Shift’s catalog sits firmly within this core, proving her music is a foundational part of the genre’s shared canon.
The Standalone Tracks: The isolated nodes floating around the perimeter represent standalone works. These are specific Oceanus Folk songs or albums connected to Sailor Shift that exist independently, without musically referencing or being referenced by the rest of the genre’s catalog.

Ultimately, this visual shifts the focus from professional networking to musical legacy, proving that Sailor Shift’s body of work is deeply embedded in the referential fabric of the Oceanus Folk genre.


2. Oceanus Folk Influence Across the Musical World
This section examines how Oceanus Folk spread across the wider musical world, whether that spread was gradual or intermittent, which genres and artists were most influenced, and how the genre itself evolved after Sailor Shift’s 2028 breakthrough.


(a) Was this influence intermittent or did it have a gradual rise?


Oceanus Folk’s influence shows a mixed diffusion pattern: it rose gradually overall, but that rise happened through intermittent spikes rather than a smooth, even increase. The left chart shows that Oceanus Folk’s outward influence on other genres was not steady year by year. In the early period, influence remained very low, with only one or two unique non-Oceanus works referencing Oceanus Folk in most years, suggesting that the genre initially had only limited reach outside its own musical space. The first clear breakthrough appears around 2017, when influenced works jump noticeably. After that, the pattern becomes much more volatile, with sharp peaks in 2023, 2027, and 2029 rather than a continuous upward climb. This indicates that the spread was intermittent in its yearly expression, with bursts of strong adoption followed by declines.
However, the cumulative chart adds an important second perspective. Although the yearly pattern is uneven, the cumulative curve rises throughout the period, especially from the early 2020s onward. This shows that Oceanus Folk’s influence was still building over time overall, even if that growth did not happen smoothly each year. The steepest cumulative growth occurs in the late 2020s, indicating that this was the most important phase in the genre’s wider diffusion across the musical world. Sailor Shift’s 2028 breakthrough sits directly within this strongest growth period. While the highest annual peak appears just before 2028, the years around her rise remain the period of greatest overall expansion, suggesting that her emergence likely coincided with and reinforced Oceanus Folk’s broader spread into other musical communities.


(b) What genres and top artists have been most influenced by Oceanus Folk?


The dashboard shows that Oceanus Folk’s influence is concentrated most strongly in a small number of genres. Dream Pop and Indie Folk emerge as the leading recipients of Oceanus Folk influence, followed by Desert Rock and Space Rock, indicating that the genre spread most successfully into a few key stylistic communities rather than evenly across the entire musical world. A second tier of adoption appears in genres such as Synthwave, Americana, and Doom Metal, while lower-count genres reflect weaker but still visible diffusion beyond the core receiving group.
At the artist and group level, the pattern is broader than it is deep. The strongest uniquely identified artist/group nodes record only a small number of distinct influenced works, showing that Oceanus Folk did not overwhelmingly shape one dominant artist community. Instead, its influence appears to have been distributed across a cluster of artists and groups, each adopting the style in a modest number of works. This suggests that Oceanus Folk spread widely across the musical world, but with its artist-level effects dispersed rather than concentrated in a few major adopters.


(c) On the converse, how has Oceanus Folk changed with the rise of Sailor Shift? From which genres does it draw most of its contemporary inspiration?


This dashboard shows that Oceanus Folk’s inspirations changed substantially after Sailor Shift’s 2028 breakthrough, becoming broader and more contemporary while still retaining a strong Indie Folk foundation. Before 2028, the genre was overwhelmingly driven by Indie Folk, with additional influence from Synthwave, Doom Metal, Synthpop, and Dream Pop, indicating a relatively concentrated stylistic base. After 2028, although Indie Folk remained the dominant influence, Oceanus Folk drew inspiration from a much wider range of genres, especially Dream Pop, Indie Rock, Alternative Rock, Psychedelic Rock, Americana, and Desert Rock. The ranking of post-2028 inspiration genres confirms that contemporary Oceanus Folk is no longer shaped by a single dominant tradition alone, but by a more varied and hybrid mix of genres. Overall, the dashboard suggests that Sailor Shift’s rise coincided with Oceanus Folk becoming more experimental, cross-genre, and musically expansive, while still remaining rooted in its original Indie Folk core.


3. Rising Star in the Music Industry


(a) Visualize the careers of three artists. Compare and contrast their rise in popularity and influence.
The three artists were not chosen randomly. They were selected to represent three different career trajectories so that the comparison could be used to define what a rising star looks like in this dataset.
Selected artists

Sailor Shift — benchmark superstar

Szymon Pyć — steady long-term builder

Sienna Fox — emerging momentum artist





The three career visualizations suggest that a rising star is not defined by output alone. A stronger profile combines three signals: growing career output, rising popularity through notable works, and expanding influence as later works begin referencing the artist’s music. In other words, a rising star is someone who not only produces more work, but also converts that output into recognition and then into broader influence.
Comparing the three artists shows three distinct career trajectories.
Sailor Shift represents the breakout benchmark. Her career output rises quickly from 2028 onward, while both her popularity and influence also grow steeply within a relatively short time window. This is the clearest breakout pattern: strong output, strong notable-work growth, and rapid accumulation of later references. She is the model of a superstar whose career accelerates quickly and whose influence expands beyond her own catalogue.
Szymon Pyć provides the contrast case of a long, steady career. His output, popularity, and influence all increase gradually across a much longer period, beginning in the late 1980s and continuing into the 2020s. Unlike Sailor Shift, his rise is not driven by a sudden surge. Instead, it is built through sustained output and cumulative recognition over decades. This makes him a useful steady-growth benchmark: successful, respected, and influential, but without the compressed upward momentum of a breakout star.
Sienna Fox represents the high-momentum emerging pattern. Her career is much shorter, but within that short span her output rises quickly, her number of notable works also grows strongly, and her influence begins appearing early. Although her total counts are still below those of Sailor Shift and Szymon Pyć, the speed of her development is important. She shows the early-stage version of a breakout profile: recent entry, fast accumulation of work, high notable-work conversion, and the beginning of influence growth.
Taken together, the three charts suggest that a rising star in this music ecosystem is best identified by rate of growth, not just career length. Sailor Shift shows the fully developed breakout model, Szymon Pyć shows long-term steady accumulation, and Sienna Fox shows the emerging high-momentum pattern that may signal the next wave of stardom.


(b) Using this characterization, give three predictions of who the next Oceanus Folk stars will be over the next five years

To make the prediction defensible, the next stars should come from the Oceanus Folk Rising Star Candidates table rather than from the comparison chart alone. That shortlist filters to solo artists, valid artist works, and recent Oceanus Folk activity, so it identifies artists who are actually emerging within Oceanus Folk itself.
Based on the rising-star profile, the next Oceanus Folk stars are predicted to be Beatrice Albright, Daniel O’Connell, and Genevieve Bell. These artists show the strongest recent momentum within Oceanus Folk, measured through recent release activity, notable works, and continued recent presence in the genre. Like Sailor Shift in her breakout phase, they combine visibility with upward trajectory, suggesting that they are the most likely acts to expand Oceanus Folk’s reach over the next five years.

title: Welcome to our Visual Analytics Project
text:
This project, part of the VAST Challenge 2025, Mini Challenge 1, aims to analyze the career of musician Sailor Shift and the rise of the Oceanus Folk genre.
Our project team is composed of:

Liew Zhen Jie (zjliew.2023@scis.smu.edu.sg)
Ewan Lim (ewanlim.2023@scis.smu.edu.sg)

Our primary objective is to develop a visual analytics website to explore: - Sailor Shift’s career development and collaborations.

The influence network of Sailor Shift and Oceanus Folk.
The evolution and spread of the Oceanus Folk genre.
Identification of potential future stars in the Oceanus Folk scene.

This website will present our analysis, visualizations, and findings.
Use the navigation bar to explore the different sections of our project.

title: Project Overview
text:
Understanding the lifecycle of a musical genre requires more than simply tracking album sales. It requires a deep structural analysis of its influence. Our motivation was to map the complete evolutionary trajectory of the “Oceanus Folk” genre. We set out to answer three core questions: How is the genre’s foundational catalogue interconnected? How did its influence historically spread to the wider music industry? And finally, can we use visual analytics to predict the next generation of breakout stars? By combining structural network topology with temporal momentum analytics, we aimed to build a comprehensive model of musical evolution. ## Issues and Problems Addressed
The core challenge of this project was untangling a dense, multi-relational knowledge graph of the music industry to assist music historians. To accurately map the ecosystem, we had to overcome several specific analytical hurdles:

Isolating Musical Legacy: The initial graph was an overwhelming hairball of professional and personal connections. The challenge was mathematically filtering the network to strip away the noise and isolate Sailor Shift’s true referential footprint, proving how her specific catalogue acts as the foundational anchor for the Oceanus Folk genre.
Temporal Data Engineering: The dataset lacked chronological start dates for individual artists, creating a barrier to tracking the genre’s evolution over time. The problem required engineering a directed-influence data model (linking artists to the release dates of their performed works) to accurately map the outward spread and shifting internal inspirations of the genre.
Overcoming the “Newcomer Blindspot”: We discovered that traditional network metrics (such as PageRank and Degree Centrality) are lagging indicators that mathematically punish emerging talent. The core predictive challenge was pivoting from structural topology to “Momentum Analytics” to identify rising stars before they establish a mature network footprint.

title: Project Overview
text:
Understanding the lifecycle of a musical genre requires more than simply tracking album sales. It requires a deep structural analysis of its influence. Our motivation was to map the complete evolutionary trajectory of the “Oceanus Folk” genre. We set out to answer three core questions: How is the genre’s foundational catalogue interconnected? How did its influence historically spread to the wider music industry? And finally, can we use visual analytics to predict the next generation of breakout stars? By combining structural network topology with temporal momentum analytics, we aimed to build a comprehensive model of musical evolution. ## Issues and Problems Addressed
The core challenge of this project was untangling a dense, multi-relational knowledge graph of the music industry to assist music historians. To accurately map the ecosystem, we had to overcome several specific analytical hurdles:

Isolating Musical Legacy: The initial graph was an overwhelming hairball of professional and personal connections. The challenge was mathematically filtering the network to strip away the noise and isolate Sailor Shift’s true referential footprint, proving how her specific catalogue acts as the foundational anchor for the Oceanus Folk genre.
Temporal Data Engineering: The dataset lacked chronological start dates for individual artists, creating a barrier to tracking the genre’s evolution over time. The problem required engineering a directed-influence data model (linking artists to the release dates of their performed works) to accurately map the outward spread and shifting internal inspirations of the genre.
Overcoming the “Newcomer Blindspot”: We discovered that traditional network metrics (such as PageRank and Degree Centrality) are lagging indicators that mathematically punish emerging talent. The core predictive challenge was pivoting from structural topology to “Momentum Analytics” to identify rising stars before they establish a mature network footprint.

title: Project Overview
text:
Approach
Our methodology fused graph-based network analysis (Gephi) with temporal data engineering (Tableau) to analyse the ecosystem across three distinct phases:

Phase 1: Internal Topology (Mapping the Music): We utilised an Ego Network filter centred on Sailor Shift intersected with the Oceanus Folk genre partition. This isolated her specific maximum reach within her community’s discography. By visualising relationships like CoverOf and DirectlySamples among these specific songs and albums, we established how her catalogue acts as a foundational anchor for a highly referential, interconnected core of music.
Phase 2: Historical Spread (Tracking the Influence): To track external evolution, we engineered a directed-influence model in Tableau. By analysing outward edges against time, we tracked how Oceanus Folk spread to outside genres (e.g., Pop, Electronic) and how its own internal inspirations shifted over time.
Phase 3: Predictive Momentum (Finding the Future): To predict rising stars, we tested traditional network metrics (PageRank) but discovered they act as lagging indicators that mathematically punish newcomers. To solve this, we pivoted to “Momentum Analytics,” identifying artists who lack mature network structures but exhibit a hyper-accelerated output of recent notable works, indicating imminent breakout status.

title: Project Overview
text:
Future Work
While this visual analytics model successfully maps the past, present, and predicted future of the Oceanus Folk ecosystem, future iterations can expand its capabilities:

The Lag-Time Coefficient: Tracking our predicted stars over the next five years of data to calculate exactly how long it takes for “Production Momentum” to translate into actual “Structural Network Centrality” (PageRank).
Granular Temporal Slicing: Transitioning from yearly aggregations to monthly or weekly time-series analyses to capture micro-trends and viral influence spikes even closer to an artist’s initial debut.
Thematic Sentiment Analysis: Integrating Natural Language Processing (NLP) into the dataset’s lyrical metadata to determine whether the highly referential core catalogue exhibits specific thematic signatures that act as catalysts for its influence on outside genres.

title: Project Proposal
text:
This project is motivated by the need to examine how Sailor Shift’s rise from a local Oceanus Folk artist to an internationally recognised musician reflects broader processes of artistic influence, collaboration, and genre transformation. Her career provides a valuable case through which to study not only individual success, but also the ways in which a regional musical tradition can gain wider cultural visibility and reshape the contemporary music landscape.
The study is further motivated by the analytical potential of knowledge graph data, which captures relationships among artists, albums, songs, genres, collaborations, and influences over time. By applying visual analytics to this interconnected data, the project seeks to produce a clearer understanding of Sailor Shift’s development, the spread of Oceanus Folk, and the conditions that may give rise to the next generation of prominent artists within this musical community.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Project Objective
The objective of this project is to develop a visual analytics website that examines Sailor Shift’s career trajectory, analyses the spread and evolution of Oceanus Folk, and identifies artists who demonstrate strong potential to emerge as future Oceanus Folk stars.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Research Questions

How did Sailor Shift’s career develop over time in terms of collaborations, genre transitions, and growth in prominence?
Which artists and genres influenced Sailor Shift, and whom has she directly or indirectly influenced in return?
How has Oceanus Folk spread across the wider music landscape, and did this influence develop gradually or through key turning points?
Which genres and major artists have been most significantly influenced by Oceanus Folk?
How has Oceanus Folk evolved during Sailor Shift’s rise, and which genres now shape its contemporary form?
What characteristics define a rising star within this music ecosystem, and which three artists appear most likely to become the next prominent Oceanus Folk stars over the next five years?

title: Storyboards
text:
In this section, we will present the storyboards that guide the user experience of our visual analytics tool.
Our storyboards will illustrate how users can interact with the visualizations to gain insights into the music knowledge graph.

title: Storyboards
section: User Scenario: Identifying a Breakout Star
text:
User Scenario: Identifying a Breakout Star
The following storyboard outlines how a music historian interacts with our dashboard to discover the next generation of Oceanus Folk artists.






Step 1: The user filters the timeline to post-2030.







Step 2: The dashboard updates, and the user sorts by Notable Works.









Step 3: The user selects ‘Min Qin’ to isolate her trajectory.







Step 4: Hovering reveals the tooltip detailing her specific momentum metrics.
